▶ Live platform

GeoFly NEXUS — AI-enhanced 3D geospatial intelligence

A single deep-learning model turns raw aerial imagery and LiDAR into a live, browsable urban-intelligence platform — per-pixel land cover, five derived analytical layers, and per-tile reporting, deployed to the public web at production scale.

89.42%
mIoU · v4 model, 7 classes
96.11%
pixel accuracy · 3 cm GSD
~352 km²
deployed across 2 regions
~€2.18/mo
steady-state cloud cost

The work

A modified SegFormer-B3 transformer performs 7-class land-cover segmentation on a custom 5-channel input — RGB + Near-Infrared + a normalised Digital Surface Model — giving the model both spectral and elevation cues to separate look-alike classes such as water vs. dark asphalt.

The same v4 checkpoint was deployed to a second region (Saarland) with no retraining and no code changes; each region is configured by a single YAML file. Outputs are served as TMS tile pyramids on AWS S3 + CloudFront through OGC services to a CesiumJS 3D viewer.

Results

  • Monotonic gains over four error-driven training rounds: 67.9 → 80.8 → 87.5 → 89.4% mIoU
  • Six of seven classes exceed 86% IoU; tree/canopy is the honest weak point at 3 cm
  • Water recovered from 0% → 93.5% IoU after targeted harbour relabelling

Model code and datasets are proprietary to GeoFly GmbH; the live platform and case study are shared for demonstration.

Radar · Sentinel-1 · ground deformation
Technical report (PDF)

PS-InSAR — La Palma volcanic deformation

Millimetre-scale ground motion from space: Persistent-Scatterer InSAR on a four-year Sentinel-1 stack, tracking surface deformation across La Palma through the 2021 Cumbre Vieja eruption.

+30 mm/yr
peak uplift · Cumbre Vieja ridge
~110
Sentinel-1 SLC acquisitions
4 yrs
2020 – 2023 time series
GNSS + EGMS
independently validated

The work

A full SNAP processing chain — TOPSAR split, precise-orbit, ESD coregistration, interferogram formation with DEM topographic-phase removal — feeding StaMPS for persistent-scatterer selection, phase unwrapping and weeding. Atmospheric delays and orbital ramps were removed to isolate true displacement.

Results

  • Uplift concentrated on the south-western ridge, consistent with magma intrusion during the eruption
  • LOS series shows a stable trend, a sharp step at the eruption, and continued uplift into 2023
  • Non-deforming coasts show no motion after correction — a clean internal validation
Optical satellite · Google Earth Engine
Notebook

NDVI change detection — Colombian Amazon reserve

An end-to-end Earth Engine workflow for vegetation-change detection over the Yaguará II reserve — and a study in doing optical change detection honestly.

312 km²
AOI · real reserve geometry
2014–2024
Landsat-8 + Sentinel-2
ΔNDVI −0.15
candidate event, ~Jan 2022
2 sensors
kept separate, never blended

The work

Sentinel-2 and Landsat-8 are kept separate so a sensor-bandpass difference is never mistaken for real change. Per-pixel cloud masking and monthly median composites suppress tropical cloud noise, and a sustained-decline test flags persistent drops rather than single noisy steps.

Results — reported honestly

  • The strongest sustained signal (~Jan 2022) recovers afterwards — reported as a candidate, not confirmed loss
  • The Landsat→Sentinel offset is never reported as a trend
  • A one-month cloud outlier is correctly ignored, not flagged as deforestation
SAR–optical fusion · agriculture · GEE · 2021 data
View on GitHub

CropPulse — operational crop monitoring

Radar sees through clouds. CropPulse uses Sentinel-1 to reconstruct the Sentinel-2 vegetation curve on cloudy days — turning gappy optical data into a continuous, operational crop monitor across seven crops in Lower Saxony.

0.65
CV R² · radar→NDVI cloud-gap-fill
0.97
spatial-CV acc. · 7 crops (0.98 random)
7
crop types · 210 fields
2018 + 22
German droughts re-detected

The work

A Google Earth Engine + Python pipeline fusing Sentinel-2 (optical), Sentinel-1 (radar) and ERA5-Land (weather). The centerpiece: a gradient-boosted model learns to predict optical NDVI from radar backscatter and fills the cloud gaps to produce a continuous greenness curve — the exact problem operational LAI services solve.

Those gap-filled curves then drive four services: per-field phenology, 7-crop classification, an ERA5 drought monitor, and a season-integrated-NDVI yield proxy.

Results

  • Per-crop gap-fill R² spans 0.81 (maize) to 0.38 (barley) — strongest where canopy structure drives radar
  • Classification holds at 0.97 under spatial cross-validation (0.98 random) — errors only between phenologically similar crops
  • Recovers seven correct crop calendars, incl. barley harvested before wheat

Built honestly: LAI is an empirical proxy and scope is one AOI / one year; classification is validated with spatial cross-validation (0.97, geographic-block hold-out), and calibrated yield is the next step.
